questions.question: What was the officer responsible for looking after military stores during the reign of the Gupta Empire called?
questions.solution:
Gupta Military Administration

In the Gupta Empire, the Ranabhandagarika was the officer in charge of the military stores or the arsenal. This role was crucial for maintaining the supply of weapons and equipment for the imperial army.

  • Pilupati: Officer in charge of the elephants.
  • Mahapratihara: Chief usher or guardian of the palace gates.

questions.question: Who handled judicial matters in the provinces during the reign of the Gupta Empire?
questions.solution:
Provincial Justice

The Uparika was the head of a provincial unit called a 'Bhukti.' Besides general administration, the Uparika held significant judicial powers within their province, acting as a representative of the King's authority at the regional level.

questions.question: What was the name of Ramagupta's widow whom Chandragupta II marry after defeating the Saka king?
questions.solution:
Marriage of Chandragupta II

According to historical and literary traditions (like the play Devichandraguptam), Chandragupta II killed the Saka king and his own brother Ramagupta to rescue and marry Dhruvadevi, who then became his chief queen.

questions.question: During the Gupta Empire, the terms 'Chata' and 'Bhata' were used for ________.
questions.solution:
Chata and Bhata

In Gupta inscriptions, the terms Chata and Bhata refer to regular and irregular troops. These terms often appear in land grant charters, where the king exempts certain lands from the entry or interference of these soldiers to protect the inhabitants.

questions.question: Whom did Chandragupta II marry after defeating the Saka king?
questions.solution:
Matrimonial Alliances

Chandragupta II followed a policy of matrimonial alliances to strengthen his empire. He married Kubernaga, a princess of the Naga dynasty. This alliance helped him secure his flank while he prepared to campaign against the Western Kshatrapas (Sakas).

questions.question: Who was the Saka Kshatrapa king defeated by Chandragupta II?
questions.solution:
Defeat of the Sakas

Chandragupta II, also known as Vikramaditya, brought an end to the Saka rule in Western India by defeating Rudrasimha III. This victory allowed the Gupta Empire to gain access to the profitable ports of the Arabian Sea.

questions.question: Who succeeded Chandragupta I as the ruler of the Gupta Empire?
questions.solution:
Succession after Chandragupta I

Samudragupta succeeded his father, Chandragupta I. Known as the 'Napoleon of India' due to his extensive military conquests, he was chosen by his father as the most worthy successor among his sons.

questions.question: Who was the son and successor of Chandragupta II?
questions.solution:
Successor of Chandragupta II

Kumaragupta I was the son and successor of Chandragupta II. His reign was generally peaceful and prosperous, marked by the performance of the Ashvamedha sacrifice and the possible foundation of Nalanda University.

questions.question: The 'Tirabhukti' mentioned in Gupta inscriptions corresponds to present-day ______.
questions.solution:
Tirabhukti Region

The term Tirabhukti mentioned in Gupta era seals and inscriptions refers to the province of North Bihar. The administrative center was located at Vaishali.

questions.question: In the Harappan civilisation, cire perdue or lost wax process was used to make statue of which material?
questions.solution:
Lost Wax Technique

The cire perdue (lost wax) process was used by Harappan craftsmen to create intricate bronze statues. This involved making a wax model, coating it in clay, melting the wax out, and pouring molten metal into the hollow mold.

questions.question: What kind of tools were mainly used by the Harappans?
questions.solution:
Harappan Tool Technology

The Harappan civilization belonged to the Bronze Age. Therefore, their tools were predominantly made of bronze (an alloy of copper and tin) and stone. They did not have knowledge of iron tools.

questions.question: What was the main purpose of the citadel in Harappan cities?
questions.option_3: To function as a residential area
questions.option_4: To organise administrative and public functions
questions.solution:
The Citadel

Harappan cities were typically divided into a lower town and a raised Citadel. The Citadel housed important administrative and public buildings such as the Great Bath, the Granary, and possibly the residences of the ruling elite.

questions.question: What was a unique feature of Harappan town planning?
questions.solution:
Grid Pattern Planning

A hallmark of Harappan urbanism was the grid pattern. Streets and lanes were laid out following a north-south and east-west alignment, intersecting at right angles, which facilitated an organized drainage system.

questions.question: Which of the following materials was not used for making ornaments during the Harappan civilisation?
questions.solution:
Materials for Ornaments

Harappans used a wide variety of materials including ivory, carnelian, gold, and steatite to make jewelry. However, there is no evidence of diamond being used during this period.

questions.question: Harshavardhana belonged to which ancient region?
questions.solution:
Harshavardhana's Origin

Harshavardhana originally belonged to the kingdom of Thanesar (in modern-day Haryana). After the death of his brother-in-law, the ruler of Maukhari, he merged the two kingdoms and moved his capital to Kannauj.

questions.question: 'The Flying Apsara' painting is a masterpiece in which of the following caves?
questions.solution:
Flying Apsaras of Ajanta

The Ajanta Caves in Maharashtra contain some of the finest examples of ancient Indian mural painting. The 'Flying Apsara' is a celebrated masterpiece located in Cave No. 17, showcasing delicate brushwork and expressive movement.

questions.question: The Indo-Greek king who issued coins with bilingual inscriptions was:
questions.solution:
Indo-Greek Coinage

Demetrius was among the first Indo-Greek kings to issue bilingual coins, featuring Greek on the obverse and Kharosthi script on the reverse. This reflected the multicultural nature of his kingdom.

questions.question: Demetrius, a Bactrian-Greek ruler, came into conflict with ______.
questions.solution:
Shunga-Greek Conflict

Demetrius (or his generals) is believed to have invaded the Indo-Gangetic plain and come into conflict with Pushyamitra Shunga, the founder of the Shunga dynasty who had overthrown the Mauryas.

questions.question: Which material was used to make the famous 'Dancing Girl' statue that belonged to the Indus Valley Civilization?
questions.solution:
The Dancing Girl

The 'Dancing Girl' is a 10.5 cm high bronze statuette found in Mohenjo-daro. It is considered a masterpiece of Harappan art, demonstrating advanced metallurgical skills and a sophisticated sense of aesthetics.

questions.question: Which Harappan site shows evidence of ploughed fields?
questions.solution:
Evidence of Agriculture

Evidence of ploughed fields, specifically showing a grid of furrows, was discovered at Kalibangan in Rajasthan. This indicates that the Harappans practiced organized agriculture.

questions.question: Which site is known for its elaborate dockyard structure?
questions.solution:
The Lothal Dockyard

Lothal is famous for possessing the world's earliest known dockyard. This brick structure was connected to the Sabarmati River and allowed ships to be berthed and loaded, proving Lothal's status as a major trading port.

questions.question: Which river is associated with the Mohenjo-daro site?
questions.solution:
Geography of Mohenjo-daro

The archaeological site of Mohenjo-daro is located on the right bank of the Indus River in the Larkana district of Sindh, Pakistan. Its name literally translates to 'Mound of the Dead'.

questions.question: The sculpture of bearded man, found in Mohejo-daro, is made of which material?
questions.solution:
The Bearded Man

The famous sculpture of the 'Bearded Man' (often called the Priest-King) found at Mohenjo-daro is made of steatite (soapstone). It depicts a figure with a trimmed beard and a headband, wearing a shawl decorated with a trefoil pattern.

questions.question: A large number of terracotta toys were found at which site of the Indus Valley Civilization?
questions.solution:
Terracotta Artifacts

A vast quantity of terracotta toys, including figurines of animals, carts, and whistles, were excavated at Mohenjo-daro. These provide insights into the daily life and recreational activities of the Harappan people.

questions.question: Which animal/bird was commonly depicted on Harappan seals?
questions.solution:
Harappan Seal Motifs

The most common animal depicted on Harappan seals is the Unicorn (a mythical one-horned creature). Other animals include the hump-less bull, rhinoceros, tiger, and elephant.
